tweets.text:
Goldman Delta 1 head: 

"The most interesting thing I read over the weekend came from Brian Armstrong at Coinbase. The accompanying chart showed AI spend has almost halved while token usage continues to grow exponentially. As Armstrong put it, “The goal isn’t to suppress usage. It’s to build the infrastructure that makes exponential growth sustainable.”  The key wasn’t using less AI, but routing. Simple tasks are  automatically sent to cheaper local or open-weight models, while  frontier models are reserved for genuinely difficult reasoning. As he  put it, “Ultimately, humans shouldn’t be choosing models. AI can automate this task.” I think this is one of the clearest examples yet of inference cost deflation. Some enterprises will build on-prem infrastructure, but many  will simply route workloads to lower cost providers. The common  denominator is that useful output per dollar keeps rising. Ironically,  if companies discover they can achieve the same output for half the AI  spend, the first instinct may not be to accelerate capex… it may be to pause and optimize."

tweets.text:
Strait of Hormuz over the last 24 hours.

I am seeing fewer vessels transiting in general (possible with AIS off). But there's an interesting point in the video from @MarineTraffic where it is clear that the US escorted two groups of tankers through the Oman lane.

This time, they brought in 3 VLCCs.

In terms of inbound tankers, this group represented half of the total inbound and was entirely on the crude side.

Given that the Iranians have said that no technical talks will take place this week, I suspect this is another move to escalate the issue of the Oman lane.

I've included both June 28 and June 29 tables as the cutoff date might confuse some people as to why Felicity or Norita were not included on the June 29 list.

Yes I said I'm bored and done posting about $MSTR.  But after today's news I'll write down my thoughts as an epilogue to my long term view and then hopefully ignore it going forward. 

For those who care about $MSTR today was a clear message of what the company is likely to do going forward.

On Friday the right side of the balance sheet was worth 90% of the left side.  

Today it is now worth exactly the same as the left side.  

My view has always been that the right side and left side should always be worth the same and any deviation is a trade.   

Within the right side of the balance sheet there is the equity and the preferred and the converts. The converts are now all likely to get put in the next three years unless BTC goes up a lot so for the moment they are essentially cash.  So it's all about the preferreds price relative to the  common.  Both rally when BTC goes up and down when down. But absent a change in BTC the preferred rallying will come at the expense of the common and paying dividends will come at the expense of the common. 

What is the company prepared to do.

1. They made a token increase in the dividend which shows a really weak commitment to the preferred going to par

2. They announced a preferred repurchase plan which can only be funded by selling BTC or selling common and it was small at less than 10% of the outstanding preferred notjonal.  Once again weak sauce. 

3. They said words that they have a corporate priority to have it trade at par.  Which is just an empty promise 

4. If it trades at par they will definitely issue more. 

On the common they will sell when the right side of the balance sheet is worth more than the left to raise cash for dividends, fund preferred buy backs and perhaps to buy BTC.  They also announced a buyback facility for the common (less than 4% of the outstanding) so when the common and capital structure is cheap to the left side and cheap to the preferred they will likely buy some. 

The big takeaway is MSTR has sold 1.2BN ish  common to get its house stable and now is in the business of keeping the whole capital structure including the amount of BTC stable.

They will sell BTC and common to save the preferred when it's in stress. They will sell common when the right side of the balance sheet is rich to buy BTC or preferred.  They will sell BTC or common to fund the dividends. 

They will sell infinite preferred at 100 to buy BTC 

They will sell BTC to save the common and pay dividends on preferred when issuance markets are in distress

All of tbis stuff is designed to maintain a stable MNAV which should be clear to anbody now is at MNAV 1.0. 

To be honest this is how a leveraged closed end fund operates.  

BUT this is the end of this company being able to expand its MNAV and out perform a leveraged etf.  That ended today

I made some quick money flipping the preferred and gun to my head the preferred has a few points of upside which should come at the expense of the common OR will rally if BTC rallies

I am neutral BTC and a buyer at 42069 so don't have a view short term on leveraged ETF's on this asset.

tweets.text:
History is made, and in such a dramatic fashion !
Gary Lineker, the famous British broadcaster and former England football star, is often quoted as having said back in 1990:
“Football is a simple game. Twenty-two players chase a ball for 90 minutes and, at the end, the Germans always win.”
Well, not today as Paraguay fought bravely and prevailed.
They scored first, then defended valiantly after Germany tied, forcing extra time and, ultimately, penalties.
For the first time in its history, Germany  — one of the favorites to win the whole thing — did not prevail in a World Cup penalty shootout … thanks to the heroics of the Paraguayan goalkeeper and a historic German miss!
What a game.
#worldcup #germany #paraguay @FIFAWorldCup @fifacom_ar

tweets.text:
Lighter is great. most traders would experience better fills there due to fees. the UI by default has far better options (more advanced order types built in for example, like an offchain smartTWAP)

Hyperliquid has way better liquidity for stock perps. for crypto majors, Lighter tends to be better. Lighter's RFQ is also quite good and partially solves the lesser stock liquidity issue. Lighter also has a different technical architecture with zk circuits; this has benefits that'll imo prove important in the future.

I will continue to use and own both. I don't have any religious/tribal view towards one or the other, don't care at all, just going wherever is best. 

Most people would have an easier time acknowledging how good Lighter is if they dropped the tribalism and just tried it/looked at the actual OB data. But it's understandable too, massive endowment effect on HYPE. Jeff/Shoku really nailed it!

(an actually balanced/objective take)

tweets.text:
Employment in AI-linked sectors is contracting:

Over the last 3 months, AI-affected sectors posted an average monthly decline of -11,000 jobs.

This includes roles in management consulting, graphic design, office administration, telephone call centers, computer systems, software publishers, and web search.

Since mid-2023, there have been only 2 months with a net increase  in employment in these sectors.

By comparison, at its 2022 peak, employment in these industries increased by as much as +55,000 jobs per month.

Furthermore, employers cited AI as the reason for 38,579 job cuts in May, the highest monthly total on record, according to Challenger Gray data.

This marks the 3rd consecutive monthly increase since ~5,000 in February.

The impact of AI on the labor market is becoming increasingly visible.

tweets.text:
US housing inventory growth is accelerating:

The monthly supply of new single-family homes rose +1.0 month in May, to 10.3 months, the highest since February 2009.

This indicator measures how many months it would take to sell all homes currently on the market at the current pace of sales.

The higher the reading, the weaker the demand relative to supply.

By comparison, the long-term median for this metric is ~6.0 months.

Since the 1970s, 6 of the 7 times this indicator surged to current levels, the US economy was already in a recession. 

This comes as elevated mortgage rates and record homeownership costs are keeping buyers on the sidelines, leaving builders with growing inventories that they are struggling to clear.

The US housing market has rarely been this oversupplied.

The problem with prediction markets is that people are using AI agents to basically out-bet you faster and earlier.

That said, part of my strategy is to use @0xbeepit AI agents as a market scanner, while keeping my position size manageable.

Beep R3 has two modes: manual swipe-style predictions and an AI Predict Agent that trades prediction markets 24/7 on autopilot.

For me, the cleaner strategy is to let the agent watch World Cup markets in the background, then keep my own rules manageable.

The real edge is identifying mispricing, not just backing teams you personally like, so the exit strategy should be decided before entering the trade.

That way, once the market starts moving, you're not panic-reacting, and you already know when to take profit or cut the position.

The group stage is over in just a few days, so it's a good time to start looking at some of the more interesting markets and taking small positions with the help of AI agents (which IMO is super simple to set up on Beep).

On top of that, they’re also running up to $500K in BEEP rewards during the World Cup Predict Arena, so if you're watching the World Cup anyway, probably worth checking out.

➡ PBOC’s overnight reverse repos is expected to become a regular liquidity-management tool, complementing seven-day operations as policymakers refine the framework and gradually improve rate transparency, report cites Wang Qing, chief macro analyst at Golden Credit Rating, as saying.

➡ The launch of the tool does not alter the PBOC’s policy-rate framework, with the seven-day reverse repo rate remaining the central benchmark guiding monetary policy, report cites Ming Ming, economist at Citic Securities.

➡ 10-year government bond futures were driven to fresh year-to-date highs as investors view the overnight reverse repos as a signal of stronger liquidity support, according to a Shanghai Securities News report, citing analysts.

*Link (Chinese): https://t.co/hbCkZrhN5r

tweets.text:
I've reached 900k followers, thank you everyone!

Just some reflection, it's actually a nightmare at higher counts...

Which is new to me, since I started at almost nothing earlier this year.

1. Threats of safety, which I don't enjoy.

- Random new accounts sending personal threats
- Mysterious foreign accounts, sending coercive messages the moment I mention a foreign ticker

If I hold an opinion about a stock, I'd prefer not to have anyone show up on the street with a knife at night since it's safety risk at this point...

Never felt like I'd have to take that into consideration for holding an opinion.

2. Egregious media narratives

- Lot of outlets try to dismiss my ideas like $RPI or $SIVE as "meme stocks" or me as "meme trader" when I talk about supply chain chokepoints or fundamental catalysts to revenue due to AI.

Without ever covering the core ideas or hyperscaler mapping I've done.

I let success validate my ideas, but it's annoying when they can just pretend their claims like "___ will imminently crash as a fact" which ends up not happening.

Or that "___" is just a retail meme stock without mentioning $JBL, Ayar, $GFS or others for CPO scale up or pluggables.

I'm grateful to some outlets, like in Belgium for $XFAB coverage, but many others try and paint their own narrative rather than covering a very nuanced thesis objectively.

- When I mention a ticker in a foreign market, some try to frame me as some "adversarial account"!

I'm just sharing ideas, and I'll always disclose positions/financial interest if I have them.

If I say I have none, they just make up some narrative that I'm some foreign institution or random group inside their country that does.

Some have even tried hilariously forging screenshots of me to harm my reputation, which was annoying to deal with. Since it's easier to textedit a photo and spread it around than it is to dispel disinformation.

Lot of institutions know me personally by now.

I just don't go broadcasting my identity to random people in the media/public, since personal security gets expensive and I just want to live about my day.

3. Endless X bot spam

- I have endless hundreds of comments a day trying to link external communities pretending they're me.

I manually delete, and block thousands of these accounts, sometimes hit regular people by accident.

And it's extremely exhausting to the point my fingers cramp certain days. 

Even if I give the most blatant warnings in bio, people still get scammed and think it's me. Then I get blamed for it too.

I actually had a conversation where someone asked if a scammer was me.

I said no verbatim, then they double checked like 2-3 more times because they didn't believe I said no because someone had the same username on another scam platform.

In another world, maybe we'll get automod type tools. But until then,  it's mentally exhausting dealing with all of this. But I do so anyways since I don't want my followers to get scammed. 

4. Endless engagement baiting

- I have tons of people making up fake stuff about me, tagging me in posts, randomly bashing my ideas while missing any technical nuance in order to get me to comment or farm views.

It's a catch 22 since the moment I reply, they get a lot of views, which was their goal. And if I don't reply it just looks like I'm avoiding something (which is not true).

I'm very transparent, I always try and entertain people who disagree first. But when it gets into personal attacks or leading questions/disinformation rather than substance then I don't engage.

It's a little sad though when people I've interacted with end up caring more about engagement over relationships down the road. 

5. People missing the nuance

- I share ideas pretty often and I always maintain I don't want people to copytrade or blindly follow, which is why I haven't set up any copytrading apps.

Before it was just posting with a few $RDDT friends on ideas like $AXTI, $NBIS, or $ALAB, and people were more knowledgeable traders. 

Then seeing if markets validate my guesses like with InP substrate bottlenecks. 

But because many turned out directionally right, to the point it became a central talking point between China/US trade relations, lot of random folks started to follow my account last few months.

But many of them miss nuances with float dilution, or material updates along the way such as rate hikes, and I get blamed if a thesis changes.

Now since there's so many random people, even if I share an idea about $CBRS last Friday.

At market open I had people panicking over a 2% drop, asking me if I did something to a $44B+ company, even if it went up 20%+ today.

Now I have compliance review for posts, which delays things a lot but makes sure people don't get the wrong idea. And I have to be careful about my timing, so I post new ideas when markets close.

Before, it was just sharing ideas the moment I had them during lunch or in the shower, so this is becoming a headache.

6. I post my core ideas for free. And I've always said this from the start.

I actually made my subscribers chat literally by accident, and set the bare minimum amount on purpose since I didn't want to take people's money. But kept it so I could avoid the bots and not spam the main timeline with random thoughts/scrapped research.

I think me becoming the #1 most subscribed to person on X was literally by accident.

But lot of weird narratives out there when media or others don't believe that I just genuinely want to share my ideas for free.

And that people shouldn't need to pay $300 in a paywalled community to see them.

_

Not sure if people want to read it all, but just some things I've gone through.

It's not as much fun at higher numbers and it would be nice to go back to when I was smaller.

Regardless, I'd rather not turn this into a job, or make it feel like one.

Will probably take a break when I hit 1M as a milestone and do something else that will help out the community like sharing my research pipelines and such.

Think a lot of my friends at 10-50K follower counts, seem to experience some of these issues too. But problems get magnified a lot at this level.

I never needed the X creator fees or anything, just wanted to share ideas for fun if people wanted to listen.

And didn't expect any popularity, but it's nice to see that people find my ideas compelling.

tweets.text:
For robotics companies, I have a favorable view on Unitree and Agility Robotics, two humanoid players. 

And I have largest concentration in Agility Robotics, since I personally prefer US humanoid players. 

For upstream component exposure, I currently own:

- Harmonic Drive (6324) given high content BOM on things like harmonic reduction gear. 
- Vishay Precision for sensors and a possible candidate for Telsa Optimus.  

LeaderDrive (688017) and Schaeffler I have favorable views on but don't own personally. 

Many of the other AI DC players, I have indirect exposure to robotics like memory. 

Don't recommend anyone to copy, just sharing personal positions/thoughts. 

Humanoid sector is large, and as seen with Goldman Sachs report that "Korean companies will command a 30% direct and indirect share of global humanoid robot production". 

Lot of players out there globally. This was an older report but just linking it again since you see all of them pop up in GS institutional reports + what they cover.

Agility Robotics is my personal favorite as of now.

tweets.text:
Coming up on 10 days living my life "dopamine-light" and it's been an incredibly eye opening experience and one that has made me feel like I am seeing the world with fresh eyes

- No shortform video content
- No video games
- No phone in the bedroom
- No watching TV while eating
- Minimal social media

I feel like a new human. My brain feels like it's healing.

The real challenge is coming up now when I am trying to slowly incorporate "work mode" again.

Trying to be semi-active on X and Discord again, while not letting my brain succumb to the infinite distractions of the infinite internet.

I've had so many people reach out over the last couple of weeks to talk about how they've been experiencing similar things that it's really feeling like an epidemic to me, hitting almost everyone (especially in my circles).

If you can.. try give yourself a few days away from the dopamine overdose and give your brain a chance to rest.

I can't recommend it highly enough.

tweets.text:
🚨 VALUATION GROUND FAULT

$DLR just paid $27M/MW at a 6.5% cap for three fully-leased AI data centers, with 3.6% escalators, 15-year leases. This is now the North Star for stabilized AI infra.

To refresh: Miners are converting Bitcoin infrastructure at $3-12M/MW. Same NOI economics (~$1.5M/MW). That's $15-24M/MW of value creation, if you can execute.

CIFR, and HUT are two public miners that have signed triple-net leases with investment-grade hyperscalers, the same lease structure as DLR's Northern Virginia portfolio. 

To see what the market thinks of their remaining opportunity, take each company's disclosed stabilized NOI (net operating income), capitalize at DLR's 6.5% rate, and subtract net debt. That's the contracted book, valued at the clearing rate $DLR just paid. (The signed leases are already funded at the project level, so no additional equity is assumed to get there.)

Subtract that from the actual market cap. The residual is what you pay for everything uncontracted: the energized capacity sitting idle (or mining BTC) and the pipeline behind it.

The highest quality part of that residual is energized but unleased MW. Power on, interconnect done, substation built, no lease yet. Even if you attribute the entire residual to just these MW and give the pipeline zero credit, the implied values are roughly:
>$2.9M/MW for CIFR's ~313 MW >$2.1M/MW for HUT's ~443 MW. 

Eight to fourteen cents on DLR's dollar for capacity that just needs a lease (not a permit).

And the gigawatts of development pipeline sitting behind all of that: Zero. The land is free. 

There is no optimism in these valuations. In a market where plenty of stocks are priced off 2030 earnings, these companies cannot get any credit for powered land they already own. 

The market is pricing the long-dated pipeline as if AI infrastructure demand stalls, lease rates collapse, and the opportunity never materializes.

Every lease signed, every MW energized, every quarter GPU rates hold is a step toward closing that gap.

$ABVX - Speculative Buy

$ABVX has what appears to be one of the most effective drugs currently being developed for ulcerative colitis. In its Phase 3 study, roughly 50% of patients achieved remission after 44 weeks compared to about 10% on placebo. If efficacy were the only consideration, the stock would likely be trading much higher.

The problem is that several cancer cases were reported during the trial.

When investors hear the word “cancer,” they immediately ask a simple question:

Does the drug cause cancer?

The market’s answer so far has been, “maybe,” which is why the stock collapsed from around $130 into double-digit territory.

But the real question is more complicated.

Ulcerative colitis patients already have an elevated cancer risk compared to the general population. Many have spent years taking powerful immune-modulating drugs. Even without ABVX, some patients in a large study would be expected to develop cancer simply because cancer occurs naturally in this population.

So the question is not whether cancer cases occurred.

The question is whether more cancer cases occurred than would normally be expected.

This is where the upcoming data becomes important.

The first part of the study already demonstrated that the drug works. The upcoming update focuses on additional patient exposure and gives investors a much larger dataset to evaluate the cancer concern.

Several analysts have attempted to model what would be required for a genuine drug-related cancer signal to emerge. Their conclusion is that one or two additional cancer cases would not be enough. Investors would likely need to see a much larger number of new cases, a clear pattern around specific cancer types, or evidence that risk increases as exposure to the drug increases.

In other words, the concern is not whether another cancer case occurs.

The concern is whether a consistent and convincing cancer pattern emerges.

So far, that pattern has not been observed.

If the upcoming data remains within the range historically seen in ulcerative colitis patients, investors may conclude that the cancer cases are related to the underlying patient population rather than the drug itself.

If that happens, attention will likely return to what made ABVX attractive in the first place: efficacy.

And the efficacy is difficult to ignore.

At $TEVA , peak sales estimates for its injectable TL1A therapy in IBD are $3-5 billion, despite the fact that Teva is expected to be second out of three competitors using the same mechanism.

ABVX’s drug is oral, with efficacy only slightly below TL1A in the available data (51% remission at 44 weeks for ABVX versus 58% for Teva). Most importantly, ABVX is the only company using its specific mechanism of action.

And as much as big pharma loves buzzwords, there are few phrases they love more than “First-in-Class” and “Best-in-Class.”

If the safety concerns prove overblown, we believe the stock could move to approximately $150 and potentially become an acquisition target at well above $200 per share.

tweets.text: When you apply the 80/20 rule to company analysis, you start by getting a threshold level of understanding – enough to see what really drives the business and what is just noise. The key 20 percent are the few data points, metrics, and insights that directly influence the core outcomes: revenue drivers, cost structures, competitive positioning, and strategic risks. Anything beyond those core elements often adds little to decision‑making because it’s just background detail. By stripping away the excess, you avoid the trap of over‑analysis and keep your focus on the mechanics that truly move the machine forward. What belongs in that remaining 20 percent depends on your specific purpose. If you’re evaluating growth, concentrate on market trends, product adoption rates, and customer lifetime value. If you’re assessing risk, zero in on leverage ratios, cash‑flow coverage, and regulatory exposure. The real question is: what are the few pieces of information that, if missing or inaccurate, would change your conclusions? Those are the ones you protect with rigorous verification and continual learning from experience because internalized insight—what you’ve seen work in practice—outweighs book knowledge alone. By consistently asking “Which variables have the highest impact on my goal?” and testing that list against historical performance, you ensure that your analysis remains lean, effective, and grounded in reality. Have you identified which specific metrics or insights form the core of your current evaluation?

thread_tweets.text: It requires judgment about what’s important and what’s not.  The key to separating the “high‑value” 20% from the easy‑but‑low‑impact 20% is to run a quick reality‑first test: identify the outcomes you truly care about, then ask which activities have the strongest causal link to those outcomes. Start by mapping cause‑effect relationships—what’s driving your goals? If an activity moves the needle on that driver, it belongs in the high‑value slice; if it only feels comfortable or convenient but doesn’t affect the driver, it’s likely just the easy 20%. Once you’ve sorted them, put the must‑do high‑value items above any “like‑to‑do” tasks and keep strategic focus front and center. In practice, I once spent months polishing a presentation deck (easy work) while the data analysis that would have shifted my investment thesis was delayed; after forcing myself to list the true outcome—accurate market insight—I realized the analysis belonged in the high‑value 20% and moved it to the top of my priority list. Apply this same diagnostic: define the ultimate result, trace the chain of effects, and rank actions by their impact on that chain, ignoring the temptation of expedient shortcuts.

The World Cup games are really something else. Beyond the competition and the games themselves—which are fantastic—there’s this incredible element of how people from different countries interact with each other.
People bring their unique personalities and nationalities, and everyone just gets along with so much respect. Whether it’s the Scots bringing their spirit and bagpipes to the games, or the Japanese fans cleaning up the stadium after a match, it’s a beautiful reflection of what the United Nations—and all countries—should be.
It brings out a joy, laughter, and a warmth that gives us a real element of optimism. We should learn from this whole experience of what it's like to be together, and how we can be together.
#WorldCup #soccer #raydalio

tweets.text:
I could not believe my eyes when I read what former Kansas City Fed President Esther George had to say in an interview today to the effect that she wouldn’t even wait till September to start hiking rates again.  And here we have Kevin Warsh at the helm, who prides himself on being an Alan Greenspan disciple (may the Maestro rest in peace).  The question is – would Greenspan really be talking about a rate hike?  He followed market indicators, not flawed government data like the CPI or PCE deflator.

As in:

(i) The yield curve:  The 2s/10s curve has flattened nearly +20 basis points since late May to just +30 basis points; (ii) The dollar:  In just five months, the DXY has strengthened nearly +6%; (iii) Credit spreads:  CCC-BB rated spreads have widened out more than +30 basis points since the end of May and are approaching 800 basis points for the first time since Liberation Day in April 2025; (iv) Commodity prices: The CRB index has rolled over by -13% from the nearby May peak; (v) Gold:  Down more than -10% in just the past month and by -27% from the early-year highs.

tweets.full_text:
It's rare in DC for the fate of major legislation to be truly unknown, but that's where we stand with the Clarity Act right now.

As of today, there's no deal, but also no lack of effort or urgency to get there in the next few weeks. July is the final stretch: it's now or never.

The main blocker is ethics. There's no path to 60 votes in the Senate unless Dems get some sort of provision that restricts the President, and maybe his family, from doing business in crypto.

Then there are lingering policy issues that were raised, but not solved, during markup.

The challenge is that there likely won't be a clear "yes" without putting the bill on the floor for a vote, but it's hard to justify using limited floor time on a bill that might not pass.

We may be in a high noise, low signal period while the work gets done behind the scenes.

@blairlmarshall For the 119th Congress.
